perturbation.original_text: (o) "Company Field" means the design, development, manufacture, marketing, promotion, advertising, sourcing, distribution and sale of solid hardwood and engineered wood flooring products by or for any Company Entity.
perturbation.changed_text: (o) "Company Field" means the design, research, import, marketing, promotion, advertising, sourcing, distribution and retail of any flooring products by or for any Company Entity.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Company Field' as activities related to hardwood and engineered wood flooring, specifically 'manufacture' and 'sale'. The modified text broadens this definition to include 'any flooring products' replacing  'manufacture' for 'research' and 'sale' for 'retail'. This creates a contradiction because the agreement later relies on this definition to delineate the scope of licensed IP. Due to the perturbation, the license could now apply to products beyond hardwood flooring, which conflicts with the original intent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a perpetual, non- exclusive, royalty-free license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Copyrights for use in the Company Field throughout the world.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Company Field' as limited to 'solid hardwood and engineered wood flooring products'. The changed text expands the definition of 'Company Field' to include 'any flooring products'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which grants a license for use 'in the Company Field'. Because the 'Company Field' definition has been broadened, the scope of the license in the contradicted text has also been broadened beyond the original intent (hardwood and engineered wood). This creates a direct conflict regarding the scope of the license, resulting in a strong in-text contradiction and making it impossible to define what the Company Field actually encompasses.

perturbation.original_text: The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.changed_text: The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall use reasonable efforts to ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be of similar quality to goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have a positive effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the Company to ensure that goods and services are 'substantially the same as or greater than' the prior quality, and should not have an 'adverse effect'. The modified text weakens the obligation to 'use reasonable efforts' to maintain 'similar quality' and should not have a 'positive effect'. This introduces ambiguity regarding the quality standards the Company must adhere to and implies to have a positive effect in Arizona's Licensed Trademarks. This contradicts the goal of maintaining consistent quality and protecting the trademarks.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall use reasonable efforts to ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be of similar quality to goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have a positive effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated a high standard of quality ('substantially the same as or greater than') and prohibited any 'adverse effect'. The changed text weakens the quality standard to 'similar quality' with 'reasonable efforts' and changes 'adverse effect' to 'positive effect'. The contradicted text is the same as the Changed text. This creates a direct contradiction because the original intent, as evidenced in the explanation, was to preserve a specific quality level and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks. The change to 'positive effect' implies that the Company can take actions that improve Arizona's Licensed Trademarks, conflicting with the intention of the parties to maintain the status quo of the trademarks.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall within the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within forty-five (45) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators. In the event that the Company or Buyer elects to institute such Proceedings, Arizona will reasonably cooperate with the Company or Buyer in such Proceedings, and the Company or Buyer shall reimburse Arizona for all reasonable costs and fees incurred by Arizona as a result of such cooperation. Such cooperation by Arizona will include joining such Proceeding as a party, if deemed necessary by the Company or Buyer. In the event that Arizona elects to bring a Proceeding against any alleged infringer of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks and seeks the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement in such Proceeding, Arizona will take reasonable steps to assist the Company or Buyer in requesting the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement, and pursuing an infringement claim against such alleged infringer. The Company or Buyer, as applicable, shall retain all benefits, recoveries, injunctions or other value derived from such Proceedings instituted by such Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall outside the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within forty-five (45) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators. In the event that the Company or Buyer elects to institute such Proceedings, Arizona will have no obligation to cooperate with the Company or Buyer in such Proceedings, and the Company or Buyer shall not reimburse Arizona for any costs and fees incurred by Arizona. Such cooperation by Arizona will not include joining such Proceeding as a party. In the event that Arizona elects to bring a Proceeding against any alleged infringer of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks and seeks the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement in such Proceeding, Arizona will obstruct assisting the Company or Buyer in requesting the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement, and pursuing an infringement claim against such alleged infringer. The Company or Buyer, as applicable, shall not retain all benefits, recoveries, injunctions or other value derived from such Proceedings instituted by such Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text grants the company the right to take action against patent infringers if Arizona declines, provided the infringement falls within the 'Company Field'. Arizona is obligated to cooperate and be reimbursed for costs. Modified: The trigger for the company's right to sue is changed to infringements 'outside the Company Field', Arizona has no obligation to cooperate and, contrarily, obstructs them from the trademark infringement claim, and the company doesn't retain any benefits. This creates a contradiction: why would the company be allowed to sue for actions outside its defined business area, and why would Arizona actively hinder an infringement claim? It undermines the intended protection of IP within the agreed Company Field.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As between the Parties, Arizona, with respect to the Arizona Licensed IP, and the Company or Buyer, with respect to the Company Licensed IP, shall have the right (but not the obligation) to elect to bring a Proceeding or enter into settlement discussions regarding, or otherwise seek to resolve, any infringement, misappropriation, or other violation, or allegations of invalidity or unenforceability, of the Licensed IP. In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall outside the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within forty-five (45) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators. In the event that the Company or Buyer elects to institute such Proceedings, Arizona will have no obligation to cooperate with the Company or Buyer in such Proceedings, and the Company or Buyer shall not reimburse Arizona for any costs and fees incurred by Arizona. Such cooperation by Arizona will not include joining such Proceeding as a party. In the event that Arizona elects to bring a Proceeding against any alleged infringer of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks and seeks the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement in such Proceeding, Arizona will obstruct assisting the Company or Buyer in requesting the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement, and pursuing an infringement claim against such alleged infringer. The Company or Buyer, as applicable, shall not retain all benefits, recoveries, injunctions or other value derived from such Proceedings instituted by such Party.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a strong in-text contradiction. The original text gives the Company or Buyer the right to institute proceedings against infringers within the Company Field, while the changed text gives that right for infringements *outside* the Company Field. This contradicts the initial premise of the agreement, which is to protect the Company's IP *within* its defined field. Further, Arizona's changed obligation to 'obstruct assisting' the Company in trademark infringement claims when the initial agreement contemplated reasonable cooperation introduces a clear conflict. The contradiction is not minor or open to interpretation. A reasonable reader would immediately recognize that allowing the company to sue for actions outside its field while simultaneously obstructing their efforts to protect the trademark is directly incompatible with the intended protection of IP within the Company Field.
